does: This paper develops AgentSeer, an observability tool that decomposes agentic LLM executions into action-component graphs to reveal vulnerabilities at both model and agent levels. The authors conduct cross-model validation using GPT-OSS-20B and Gemini-2.0-flash against HarmBench jailbreaks under single-turn and iterative-refinement attacks, quantifying the gap between traditional model-level risk assessment and actual agent-level vulnerability. They demonstrate that iterative prompt hardening can be automated using action-graph risk signals to mitigate both direct and iterative jailbreak attacks. Results show significant differences in vulnerability profiles: baseline model-level attack success rates differ between models (39.47% for GPT-OSS-20B versus 50.00% for Gemini-2.0-flash), with both exhibiting susceptibility to social engineering, though agent-level assessment reveals deployment-specific risks invisible to standard evaluation.
connects: This work addresses a critical gap in the evaluation of LLMs deployed as autonomous agents, extending prior research on jailbreaking and safety evaluation that focused primarily on model-level risks without considering agentic execution contexts. It builds on established jailbreaking methodologies like HarmBench and iterative refinement attacks while incorporating observability and monitoring concepts from systems engineering. The paper connects to recent work on adversarial robustness in language models and automated defense mechanisms like prompt hardening, responding to the recognized problem that safety evaluations of isolated models do not capture deployment-specific risks. It also extends work on understanding model vulnerabilities through systematic evaluation frameworks, applying these insights specifically to the emerging domain of autonomous LLM agents.
enables: This work enables more comprehensive safety evaluation and deployment practices for agentic LLM systems by providing both visibility and automated mitigation tools. The action-graph decomposition methodology could be adapted to other agentic architectures and multi-step reasoning systems, supporting the development of agent-specific safety benchmarks beyond model-level metrics. The automated prompt hardening approach demonstrated here could inform the design of runtime defense mechanisms for production LLM agents. Future work could extend this to multi-agent scenarios, longer-horizon tasks, and explore whether agent-level vulnerabilities reveal fundamental limitations requiring architectural changes rather than defensive prompting. The observability framework also opens possibilities for developing more sophisticated risk quantification methods and for understanding how agent design choices interact with underlying model vulnerabilities.
